items.prompt_md: What does **amortized** time complexity actually describe?
options: The average cost per operation over a long sequence, even though a few individual operations may be much more expensive than that average
The cost of the single most expensive operation in the sequence, ignoring all the cheap ones
The best-case cost of an operation, assuming the input is already sorted or nearly empty
The cost of an operation measured only on the very first call, before any resizing has happened
items.answer: The average cost per operation over a long sequence, even though a few individual operations may be much more expensive than that average
items.explanation_md: Amortized analysis spreads the cost of occasional expensive operations across the many cheap ones around them, so the *average* per-operation cost stays low even though no single operation is guaranteed to be cheap.

items.prompt_md: A dynamic array's `.append()` doubles the backing array's capacity whenever it's full, copying every existing element into the new array. Why is `.append()` still considered **O(1) amortized**, not O(n)?
options: Because the expensive doubling step happens only O(log n) times over n appends, so the total copying work across all n appends is still O(n), which averages out to O(1) per call
Because copying an array is always an O(1) operation regardless of how many elements it contains
Because the doubling step is skipped entirely once the array has been resized once
Because amortized analysis only counts the cheapest operation and discards the expensive ones from the total
items.answer: Because the expensive doubling step happens only O(log n) times over n appends, so the total copying work across all n appends is still O(n), which averages out to O(1) per call
items.explanation_md: Doublings become exponentially rarer as the array grows, so their total copying cost across n appends sums to O(n) — spread over n calls that's O(1) per call on average, even though any single append that triggers a resize costs O(n) by itself.

items.prompt_md: You're told an operation is "O(1) amortized." What does that guarantee about any *one particular* call to that operation?
options: Nothing — that one call could still take O(n) time; the guarantee only holds for the average over a long sequence of calls
That specific call is guaranteed to take O(1) time, no exceptions
That specific call is guaranteed to take exactly O(log n) time
That specific call will never touch more than one element of the underlying structure
items.answer: Nothing — that one call could still take O(n) time; the guarantee only holds for the average over a long sequence of calls
items.explanation_md: Amortized bounds are a statement about totals over a sequence of operations, not about any individual operation. An individual append that triggers a resize can still cost O(n) on its own; amortized O(1) just means that cost is rare enough not to raise the average.

items.prompt_md: What exactly does **space complexity** measure in most Big-O discussions of an algorithm?
options: The extra (auxiliary) memory the algorithm uses beyond the input itself, as a function of input size
The total memory used by the input plus the algorithm, always expressed as O(n) at minimum
The number of variables declared anywhere in the source code, regardless of input size
The peak CPU cache size needed to run the algorithm without a cache miss
items.answer: The extra (auxiliary) memory the algorithm uses beyond the input itself, as a function of input size
items.explanation_md: Space complexity is about *extra* memory — the input array or string you were handed doesn't count against you; what counts is whatever additional structures (arrays, hash maps, call stacks) your approach allocates on top of it.

items.prompt_md: An approach keeps two running scalar variables (a running minimum and a running best-so-far) while scanning an input of size n once. What is its space complexity?
options: O(1) — a fixed number of scalars regardless of n is constant extra space
O(n) — any variable that changes value during the scan counts as O(n) space
O(log n) — because the values themselves can grow as large as the input range
O(n^2) — one unit of space per pair of elements compared
items.answer: O(1) — a fixed number of scalars regardless of n is constant extra space
items.explanation_md: Two running numbers is a constant amount of extra memory no matter whether the input has 10 elements or 10 million — that's the definition of O(1) space. The values themselves changing doesn't add new memory; you're still only ever storing two numbers.

items.prompt_md: Two solutions both run in O(n) time on an input of size n. Solution A only tracks a couple of running variables. Solution B first builds a whole auxiliary array of size n (e.g. running minimums at every index) before scanning it for the answer. How do they compare on space?
options: A is O(1) extra space; B is O(n) extra space — building a second array the size of the input costs memory even if it doesn't cost extra time
They're identical in space, since both are O(n) in time and space always matches time
A is O(n) extra space and B is O(1), because B discards the array once it finds the answer
Both are O(1) extra space, since the input itself is not counted either way
items.answer: A is O(1) extra space; B is O(n) extra space — building a second array the size of the input costs memory even if it doesn't cost extra time
items.explanation_md: Time and space are separate axes. Both solutions can be O(n) time, but B allocates a whole extra array sized to the input, which is O(n) *extra* space, while A only ever holds a constant number of scalars — O(1) space.

items.prompt_md: Which phrase in a problem description is the strongest cue that an O(1)-extra-space solution is likely reachable?
options: "You only need the best/min/max seen so far" — implying you can track a running summary instead of storing the whole history
"Return all valid combinations that satisfy the condition" — implying every result must be collected
"The input may contain up to 10^5 elements" — implying a large input size
"Sort the array before processing it" — implying an auxiliary sorted copy is required
items.answer: "You only need the best/min/max seen so far" — implying you can track a running summary instead of storing the whole history
items.explanation_md: Needing only a running summary (a min, a max, a best-so-far) rather than the full history of everything seen is exactly the signal that a couple of scalar variables can replace a whole auxiliary array, pointing toward O(1) extra space.

items.prompt_md: A recursive function calls itself once per element, reaching a maximum recursion depth of n before any call returns. Why does this cost O(n) space even if the function allocates no arrays or hash maps of its own?
options: Because each active call frame sits on the call stack until it returns, so a recursion depth of n means n stack frames are alive at once
Because recursive functions always allocate a new array of size n internally, whether or not the code shows it
Because Python (and most languages) pre-allocates O(n) memory before any function is called, regardless of recursion
Recursion depth has no effect on space; only explicitly declared variables count toward space complexity
items.answer: Because each active call frame sits on the call stack until it returns, so a recursion depth of n means n stack frames are alive at once
items.explanation_md: The call stack is real memory: every unreturned recursive call keeps its frame (local variables, return address) alive. A call chain n deep therefore holds n frames simultaneously, which is O(n) auxiliary space even without a single explicit array.

items.prompt_md: An approach uses a hash set to record every distinct value it has seen while scanning an input of size n. In the worst case (all values distinct), what's the space cost of that hash set alone?
options: O(n) — in the worst case the set can grow to hold one entry per input element
O(1) — hash sets always use a small fixed amount of memory regardless of how many entries they hold
O(log n) — hash sets only need to store the count of distinct elements, not the elements themselves
O(n^2) — hash collisions force the set to store each element multiple times
items.answer: O(n) — in the worst case the set can grow to hold one entry per input element
items.explanation_md: A hash set/map's memory grows with the number of distinct entries it holds. If every element could end up being distinct, the set can hold up to n entries, so its space cost is O(n) in the worst case.

items.prompt_md: "Trading space for time" (e.g. memoizing results in a hash map) is worth it in which situation?
options: When the same expensive computation would otherwise be redone many times, so caching results in extra memory avoids repeating that work
When memory is more scarce than time and reducing memory usage is the goal, regardless of how it affects runtime
Only when the input is already sorted, since sorting removes the need for any caching
Never — trading space for time always makes both the time and space complexity worse
items.answer: When the same expensive computation would otherwise be redone many times, so caching results in extra memory avoids repeating that work
items.explanation_md: The classic space-for-time trade is memoization: spend O(n) or more extra memory to remember answers to subproblems so you never recompute the same expensive result twice, cutting time complexity at the cost of space complexity.

items.prompt_md:
```python
def running_totals(nums: list[int]) -> int:
    total = 0
    best = float("-inf")
    for x in nums:
        total += x
        best = max(best, total)
    return best
```
What is the **space complexity** of this function (extra space beyond the input list)?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
items.explanation_md: The function only ever keeps two running scalars, `total` and `best`, no matter how long `nums` is — that's constant extra space, O(1), regardless of its O(n) time cost.

items.prompt_md:
```python
def prefix_maxes(nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    maxes = []
    running_max = float("-inf")
    for x in nums:
        running_max = max(running_max, x)
        maxes.append(running_max)
    return maxes
```
What is the **space complexity** of this function, counting the returned `maxes` list as extra space?
options: O(n) — the output array grows to hold one entry per input element
O(1) — only two variables, `running_max` and `x`, are ever tracked
O(log n) — the array only needs to store the maxima, which grow logarithmically
O(n^2) — appending to a list repeatedly costs quadratic total space
items.answer: O(n) — the output array grows to hold one entry per input element
items.explanation_md: Unlike a version that only returns a single running maximum, this one builds and keeps an entire auxiliary array the size of the input, so its space cost is O(n) — even though the time complexity is still just O(n).

items.prompt_md: A dynamic array starts empty and has `.append()` called on it n times in a row, doubling its backing capacity whenever it's full. What is the **total** time cost of all n appends combined (not per-call — the sum across the whole sequence)?
options: O(n) total, because the geometric series of doubling costs (1 + 2 + 4 + ... + n) sums to O(n)
O(n log n) total, because each doubling costs O(n) and there are O(log n) doublings, multiplied together
O(n^2) total, because every append after a doubling has to re-copy all previous elements
O(1) total, because doubling amortizes the cost down to nothing
items.answer: O(n) total, because the geometric series of doubling costs (1 + 2 + 4 + ... + n) sums to O(n)
items.explanation_md: The doubling copy costs form a geometric series (roughly 1, 2, 4, 8, ..., n) that sums to at most 2n, i.e. O(n) total work across all n appends — this is exactly why each individual append is O(1) amortized: O(n) total / n appends = O(1) average.

options: O(n) — the recursion reaches a depth of n before any call returns, keeping n stack frames alive at once
O(1) — no arrays or hash maps are declared, so the function uses no extra space
O(log n) — recursive calls always halve the remaining work
O(n^2) — each stack frame holds a copy of every previous frame's data
items.answer: O(n) — the recursion reaches a depth of n before any call returns, keeping n stack frames alive at once
items.explanation_md: Even though the function declares no explicit arrays, each unreturned recursive call keeps its own stack frame alive until the base case is hit and the calls start returning. A recursion depth of n means n live frames at the deepest point, which is O(n) auxiliary space — the hidden cost of recursion.
